metadata.seed: Not really. I mean, it's pretty much the same pace. I'm very frustrated with the whole system. I'm working with inner city kids, african american kids, males in particular. And, you know, I sit and talk with these kids and I realize that there's not much I can do for them. You know, for the most part, we're talking about, you know, criminal arrest, drug abuse, you know, no education. And, you know, I don't know what I can really do for them anymore. I mean, you know, I sit and talk to them, but really, in terms of their statistics, it's looking pretty bad for them. So, you know, I get pretty frustrated and it's kind of depressing to do this every day.

patient.query: Not really. I mean, it's pretty much the same pace. I'm very frustrated with the whole system. I'm working with inner city kids, african american kids, males in particular. And, you know, I sit and talk with these kids and I realize that there's not much I can do for them. You know, for the most part, we're talking about, you know, criminal arrest, drug abuse, you know, no education. And, you know, I don't know what I can really do for them anymore. I mean, you know, I sit and talk to them, but really, in terms of their statistics, it's looking pretty bad for them. So, you know, I get pretty frustrated and it's kind of depressing to do this every day.

automatic_thoughts: there's not much I can do for them
it's looking pretty bad for them

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck and overwhelmed by the challenges you see with these kids, and that’s understandable. You’re noticing a lot of frustration and sadness, and it can feel like there’s little you can do. It’s normal to feel that way when the situation seems so difficult.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess that's right. I just... I want to help them, but sometimes it feels like I'm hitting a wall. It’s hard to see their struggles and feel like I'm not making any difference. I get so frustrated, like, what’s the point?

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck and questioning your impact. What moments come to mind where your help made a difference, even if it seemed small? How might looking at those moments change the way you view your role?

patient.query: I don’t know, I guess there have been a few times when someone thanked me or said my support helped them. But those moments feel so rare compared to the days when I just... I feel overwhelmed. It’s like I can’t hold onto those positives when everything else feels so heavy.

triggers: someone said my support helped them

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing that moments of appreciation feel few compared to the days you feel overwhelmed. What evidence do you have that these positive moments are truly rare, and how might you remember them when you’re feeling heavy? Perhaps you could try noting one small positive moment each day, even if it feels small, to see if it changes how you feel.

patient.query: I guess I could try that, but it’s hard to think about those moments when everything feels so bleak. It’s like... my mind just zooms in on the negatives and forgets the good stuff. Sometimes I feel like I should be able to handle it better, you know? I'm just not sure if it will help.

automatic_thoughts: my mind just zooms in on the negatives
I should be able to handle it better
I'm not sure if it will help

llm_response.response: It sounds like those moments feel overwhelming and you’re noticing how quickly your thoughts shift toward the negative. Feeling unsure about whether a new approach will help is completely understandable. You’re not alone in feeling that way, and it’s okay to take things one step at a time.

patient.query: Yeah, I just... it’s like I’m stuck in this loop of doubt. I want to believe it could get better, but there’s this nagging voice telling me I won’t be able to change anything. It’s frustrating because I know I want to be better, but I feel like I’m just treading water most days.

automatic_thoughts: I won’t be able to change anything
I’m stuck in this loop of doubt

entailment: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like that inner voice is really weighing on you—what kinds of thoughts or evidence do you notice that support the idea you can’t change anything? Have there been moments, even small ones, where you’ve seen yourself shift or improve? How might you view those moments differently, or what might a supportive friend say about them?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess that voice just... I don’t know, it always makes me think of times I messed up or didn't do enough. Like, I remember when I tried to pitch an idea at work and it just fell flat. It’s hard to see anything beyond that failure. Maybe I do remember those small wins, but they often feel overshadowed by the bigger mess-ups. I can't help but think a friend would probably say to focus on the good, but it feels so much easier said

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how much that voice seems to pull you back to those moments of feeling like you fell short. What evidence do you have that those bigger mistakes outweigh the smaller successes you’ve had? How might you imagine a friend or a neutral observer weighing those same moments differently?

patient.query: I don’t know, it feels like the failures just stick with me more. They replay in my head, like a bad movie I can’t stop watching. Maybe a friend would see the whole picture and not just focus on the negatives, but it’s hard for me to do that. I keep thinking about what I should’ve done differently instead of the times when things went well. It’s like I’m my own worst critic, you know?

automatic_thoughts: friend would see the whole picture
hard to focus on the positives

neutral: Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)
Tends to fail to learn from adverse experience (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like those thoughts keep looping in a way that feels exhausting. What evidence do you have that the failures truly “stick” with you, and can you think of any moments when you noticed something positive instead? If a close friend were watching, what might they point out that you’re missing right now?

patient.query: Yeah, it definitely feels exhausting. I guess I just remember the bad stuff so vividly, like the times I felt embarrassed or didn't meet expectations. But... I mean, I can think of some times when I actually did help someone, or when my boss said I had a strong performance on a project. If my friend were here, maybe they'd remind me that I’ve gotten good feedback before and that I can learn from mistakes instead of just dwelling on them. It’s just hard to shift my focus

automatic_thoughts: I only dwell on the bad

llm_response.response: Your exhaustion feels real, especially when those vivid memories of embarrassment and unmet expectations keep surfacing. What evidence do you have that supports the idea that you’re not meeting expectations, and what evidence suggests otherwise? How might you balance those memories with the positive feedback you’ve received to help shift your focus?

patient.query: I mean, I guess I can remember the times when things didn’t go as planned, like that pitch. It stung a lot, and it’s easier to cling to that than to think about the projects I nailed or the compliments I’ve gotten. Maybe I do have a few good pieces of feedback, but they often feel overshadowed by the failures. It’s just... I wish I could hold onto the positives more easily, instead of them slipping away so fast. It’s like they get

triggers: times when things didn’t go as planned

llm_response.response: It sounds like the failures feel like they’re always there, while the positives seem to fade quickly. What evidence do you have that the positives are slipping away? Can you recall any moments when the positives stayed or had a lasting impact?

patient.query: I guess I think they fade because I don’t really celebrate them. Like, I might feel good for a moment after someone praises me, but then it’s like I forget about it the next day. I can remember a time when a colleague told me I handled a tough situation really well, but instead of feeling proud, I just brushed it off and thought, “Yeah, but what about the next thing I’ll mess up?” It’s frustrating because I know I should be able to hold onto

automatic_thoughts: I might mess up next time
I should be able to hold onto good feelings

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a gap between the compliment and your expectation to keep that feeling. What would it look like if you allowed yourself to notice and savor the positive moment for a few minutes before moving on? How might that change the way you feel about future compliments?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: belief about holding onto good feelings
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown
